coredata.dc:title: Novel hierarchical carbon quantum dots-decorated BiOCl nanosheet/carbonized eggshell membrane composites for improved removal of organic contaminants from water via synergistic adsorption and photocatalysis
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Qin
Huang, Weiya
Xu, Chong
Liu, Xin
Yang, Kai
Li, Dan
Hou, Yang
Dionysiou, Dionysios D.
coredata.dc:description:
A series of carbon quantum dots (CQDs)-decorated BiOCl/carbonized eggshell membrane (CEM) composites were synthesized with CEMs as bio-templates. The obtained composites, especially BiC-0.05, exhibited superior removal efficiencies of several organic pollutants, i.e. tetracycline hydrochloride (TC), Rhodamine B (RhB), and o-nitrophenol (NP). Approximately, 98% of TC was removed after 30 min dark adsorption and subsequent 30 min visible-light-initiated photocatalytic degradation. BiC-0.05 was also utilized to treat actual industrial wastewater and could reduce 73.1% COD, in comparison to 49.9% with the use of BiOCl. The boosted removal efficiencies were achieved by synergistic effect of adsorption and photocatalysis, where cooperative contribution of oxygen vacancies (OVs), CEMs and CQDs enabled faster charge migration and more effective separation of photogenerated charge carriers. Electron spin resonance (ESR) and trapping experimental results showed that superoxide radical (
O2 −), along with holes (h+) and hydroxyl radical (
OH) would take part in photocatalytic degradation process. The intermediates of TC and its possible degradation pathway during the photocatalytic reaction were also studied. Our work developed a novel strategy by integrating carbon nanomaterials derived from bio-waste to design efficient photocatalysts for remediation of organic contaminants via adsorption – photocatalysis synergism and OVs – CQDs cooperation.

head.ce:title: Novel hierarchical carbon quantum dots-decorated BiOCl nanosheet/carbonized eggshell membrane composites for improved removal of organic contaminants from water via synergistic adsorption and photocatalysis

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
A series of carbon quantum dots (CQDs)-decorated BiOCl/carbonized eggshell membrane (CEM) composites were synthesized with CEMs as bio-templates. The obtained composites, especially BiC-0.05, exhibited superior removal efficiencies of several organic pollutants, i.e. tetracycline hydrochloride (TC), Rhodamine B (RhB), and o-nitrophenol (NP). Approximately, 98% of TC was removed after 30 min dark adsorption and subsequent 30 min visible-light-initiated photocatalytic degradation. BiC-0.05 was also utilized to treat actual industrial wastewater and could reduce 73.1% COD, in comparison to 49.9% with the use of BiOCl. The boosted removal efficiencies were achieved by synergistic effect of adsorption and photocatalysis, where cooperative contribution of oxygen vacancies (OVs), CEMs and CQDs enabled faster charge migration and more effective separation of photogenerated charge carriers. Electron spin resonance (ESR) and trapping experimental results showed that superoxide radical (
O2 −), along with holes (h+) and hydroxyl radical (
OH) would take part in photocatalytic degradation process. The intermediates of TC and its possible degradation pathway during the photocatalytic reaction were also studied. Our work developed a novel strategy by integrating carbon nanomaterials derived from bio-waste to design efficient photocatalysts for remediation of organic contaminants via adsorption – photocatalysis synergism and OVs – CQDs cooperation.

ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalysis is known as a promising technology to remediate water which is polluted by different hazardous organic contaminants, including synthetic dyes, phenols, and emerging environmental contaminants (e.g. antibiotics) [1–5]. In particular, the utilization of clean and renewable solar energy in the semiconductor-assisted photocatalysis has been proven as a “green” solution to address two of the major challenges which are currently inhibiting the sustainable development of society, environmental pollution and energy crisis [2,3,6]. In order to trigger an efficient photocatalytic reaction and achieve a satisfactory treatment efficiency, it is necessary to induce an effective contact between target organic molecules and active species, namely photo-induced electrons or holes, on the surfaces of catalysts. Several studies have shown that the photocatalytic degradation of organic contaminants can be enhanced by improved adsorption capability of catalysts towards pollutants and especially synergistic effect of adsorption and photocatalysis [4,7–10,11].

BiOCl, as one of the promising Bi-based semiconductors, possesses some attractive advantages, such as high stability, simple preparation, and availability of raw materials [12–15]. BiOCl nanosheets have been proven to possess enhanced adsorption ability to eliminate organics, e.g. antibiotics, ascribed to the special layered structure, large surface area and rich Bi ions [8]. Meanwhile, BiOCl has shown excellent photocatalytic performance in water splitting and organic contaminant removal, which are believed to be better than traditionally used TiO2 because of its open crystalline structure [16,17]. However, the practical application of BiOCl is far from satisfied, which is conspicuously hindered by its relatively large band gap (3.19 ~ 3.44 eV) [18].

In order to narrow the band gap of BiOCl and broaden its light absorption capacity, especially to construct visible-light-driven catalyst with superior photocatalytic activity, research effort has been put forward to modify the structural and chemical properties of BiOCl [19–21]. It is noticeable that the intrinsic properties of semiconductors can be ameliorated by modulating their properties and structures, such as morphologies, exposed crystal facets, and defects [14,18]. Inspired by the unique 3D hierarchical architectures of living organisms, researchers have constructed catalysts with highly ordered hierarchical structures via utilizing natural materials as bio-templates, such as butterfly wings, eggshell membranes, and leaves [17,22,23]. Those catalysts possess finely replicated hierarchical scaffold structures of biomaterials, showing incomparable features for enhanced adsorption and accelerated photocatalytic degradation of organic contaminants [9,14]. Eggshell membranes (EMs), which are layers of protein-rich membranes between eggshells and egg white, possess a macroporous network of interwoven fibers. The interwoven fibrous structures of EMs are suitable for in situ growth of several metal oxides or sulfides, due to plenty of functional groups on the surfaces of EM fibers, such as –OH, –SH, –COOH and –NH2 [24]. Indeed, EMs are rich in carbon elements, and thereby can be used as templates and raw materials to prepare novel carbon composites with highly porous and interwoven fibrous structure. That unique carbon network is believed to promote electrical conductivity, facilitate charge transfer process, and consequently boost photocatalytic activity of the resulting material [22,24–26]. For example, Pant et al. decorated carbonized eggshell membrane (CEM) with CdS-TiO2 NPs and the obtained CdS-TiO2/CEM showed effective adsorptive and photocatalytic properties for the removal of methylene blue from water [25].

We have noted that carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a carbon material of great potential in many specific applications, have drawn tremendous research attention, owing to their excellent electron conductivity, high solubility, good biocompatibility, and up-conversion performance [27–31]. CQDs have been widely used to decorate semiconductors to improve their optical and electrical properties, and consequently achieve enhanced photocatalytic activities for the resulting composites applied in hydrogen evolution and environmental remediation [32–34]. The presence of CQDs accelerated the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, and enabled more active species of holes and
O2 − participating in the photocatalytic degradation reactions [30,34]. In particular, the “green” synthesis of CQDs from natural carbon sources, such as mushrooms, microorganisms, bamboos, etc., has generated considerable interests due to the facile preparation and minimization of chemical use. Moreover, the resulting CQDs possess some chemical groups originated from the biomaterials which in turn can enhance electron transfer capability [35]. For instance, Sargin et al. synthesized CQDs from edible mushroom via microwave irradiation and used them as sensitizers to modify TiO2. The obtained TiO2/CQDs showed better photocatalytic activity than the bare TiO2 system under visible light irradiation [32]. Taken the above literature into consideration and initiated by the motivation of “resource recycle”, it is advisable to recycle and utilize biological waste EMs to construct novel hierarchical photocatalysts for efficient treatment of organic pollutants-containing wastewater.

In this paper, the composites (CQDs/BiOCl/CEM) were synthesized by in situ growing BiOCl nanosheets on carbonized eggshell membranes (CEMs). The amount of CEMs added during synthesis was carefully regulated to optimize the design of composite for use in photocatalytic reactions under visible light irradiation; in which Rhodamine B (RhB), tetracycline hydrochloride (TC), and o-nitrophenol (NP) were selected as target organic compounds. The potential application of optimized catalyst in environmental remediation was further examined by studying the change of chemical oxygen demand (COD) from actual industrial wastewater. This work also explored the possible synergistic adsorption – photocatalysis mechanism for organic contaminant removal in detail, in which our understanding on the cooperative effect of CQDs, facets and defects of BiOCl, as well as the good electron conductivity of carbon fiber networks from CEMs, was incorporated.

Fresh discarded eggshells were collected from local cafeteria. After being washed with deionized (DI) water, the eggshells were soaked in 0.5 mol/L HCl solution overnight. The eggshell membranes (EMs) were carefully peeled off from the calcified part of eggshell, rinsed thoroughly with DI water, cut into a size of about 1 × 1 cm2, and then dried naturally. The carbonized eggshell membranes (CEMs) were obtained after the thermal treatment of EMs in nitrogen atmosphere at a heating speed of 5 °C /min and maintained at 550 °C for 2 h. The resulting black CEMs were ground to powders for further use.

The procedure of preparation of CQDs-decorated BiOCl/CEM composites was presented in Scheme 1 and detailed in Supporting Information. Typically, a proper amount of CEMs was dispersed in an aqueous solution containing Bi3+ and ethanol. The low viscosity of ethanol enabled the quick dispersion of reactants on the surfaces of CEMs. Since Bi3+ ions exist in a stable form in the alcohol-containing solution and can easily interact with oxygen-containing groups [36–38], we anticipated that the interaction of Bi3+ ions with –CO/C–OH groups on CEMs could lead to in situ growth of BiOCl nuclei. As a type of bio-templates, the unique fibrous reticular structure of CEMs enabled the preferable growth of BiOCl and then the formation of ultrathin nanosheets anchored on CEMs. Meanwhile, during the ultrasonic process, CEMs would act as carbon sources to form carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [35,36]. After the pH of mixture was finely adjusted to 7.0, the suspension was aged for 8 h to allow BiOCl, which had been loaded on CEMs, to further grow and form intercrossed nanosheets with CQDs decoration. The obtained CQDs/BiOCl/CEM composites were named as BiC-x (x = 0.01, 0.05 and 0.10), where x refers to the mass of CEMs added in the reaction system. The calculated C/Bi molar ratio in BiC-0.01, BiC-0.05, and BiC-0.10 is 0.2, 1.0 and 2.0, respectively.
The characterization of samples was detailed in Supporting Information.

The photocatalytic activities of samples were evaluated by photocatalytic degradation of our selected organic pollutants (Tetracycline hydrochloride – TC, rhodamine B – RhB, and o-nitrophenol – NP) under visible light irradiation at 25 °C. The details of photocatalytic degradation, trapping and reusability experiments were described in Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: COD removal from real industrial wastewater
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic COD removal from the sample of industrial wastewater, which was collected from Jiangxi Chemical Biopharm-tech. Co., Ltd. (Jiujiang, China), over BiC-0.05 was carried out. The same experiment was also conducted by using the bare BiOCl sample for comparison. The experimental details were included in Supporting Information.

Fig. 1 a shows the XRD spectra of bare BiOCl and CQDs/BiOCl/CEM composites (BiC-x; x = 0.01, 0.05 and 0.10); whilst Fig. S1a shows the XRD spectrum of plain CEMs. The sample of BiOCl exhibits several characteristic peaks located at 2θ of 11.99°, 25.88°, 32.53°, 33.47°, and 46.66°, corresponding to the (001), (101), (110), (102) and (200) crystal surfaces of BiOCl (JCPDS 85-0861) [4]. These characteristic peaks are also observed in all samples of BiC-x (x = 0.01, 0.05 and 0.10), indicating the successful synthesis of BiOCl in the composites. The XRD spectrum of CEMs displays a characteristic peak at 2θ = 26.04° (Fig. S1a), corresponding to the (002) crystalline surface of graphite layer and in turn suggesting the presence of carbon structure. No obvious peaks ascribed to carbon are found in the samples of BiC-x, probably because of the small amount of CEMs introduced into the composites. It is noted that the intensity ratio of (110)/(001) peak for BiC-0.05 is calculated to be 5.08, which is much higher than those of BiOCl (1.55), BiC-0.01 (1.95) and BiC-0.10 (3.59), indicating the preferable growth of BiOCl crystals along the (110) orientation [20]. Furthermore, BiC-0.05 shows apparently reduced and broadened (001) diffraction peak, as compared with pure BiOCl, indicating the formation of ultrathin nanosheets of BiOCl along the (001) crystallographic direction [39]. This suggests that BiOCl nanosheets in our synthesized BiC-0.05 sample probably have the highly exposed {0
0
1} crystal facets [40].

The FTIR spectra of BiOCl and BiC-x (x = 0.01, 0.05 and 0.10) are shown in Fig. S1b. The peaks at around 1615 and 3472 cm−1 are originated from the adsorbed H2O or surface –OH groups in the samples [41]. The absorption peak seen at around 1390 cm−1 may be ascribed to the Bi-Cl bond in BiOCl; while the characteristic peak appearing at 531 cm−1 is assigned to the Bi-O bond in BiOCl [15,41]. It confirms the presence of BiOCl in samples BiC-0.01, BiC-0.05 and BiC-0.10; this is also supported by the XRD characterization (Fig. 1a).

The textural properties of as-prepared samples were further characterized by N2 adsorption-desorption analysis, as shown in Fig. S1c. Their pore size distribution curves are demonstrated in Fig. S1d. The CQDs/BiOCl/CEM composites display type IV isotherms with H3 hysteresis loops [42], indicating the presence of mesopores [14]. The surface area of BiC-0.01, BiC-0.05 and BiC-0.10 is 17.00, 24.97 and 24.87 m2/g, respectively. As compared to BiOCl (5.44 m2/g) and CEMs (0.05 m2/g), the greater surface area of BiC-x (x = 0.01, 0.05 and 0.10) is beneficial for boosting removal efficiency of pollutants from wastewater [43,44]. In Fig. S1d, BiOCl shows a single pore size distribution at approximately 3 nm, whilst the BiC-x composites possess multimodal pore sizes with average pore sizes centered at around 2 ~ 7 nm and 30 nm. This might be the result of intercrossing of BiOCl nanosheets, as further revealed by the SEM images (Fig. 1c–f).

The Raman spectra of BiC-0.05 and CEMs are shown in Fig. S2. Two wide peaks at approximately 1350 and 1590 cm−1 can be observed in CEMs, corresponding to the D and G band of graphite structures [45]. Two weak peaks assigned to the D and G band are also seen in BiC-0.05, as a result of the presence of CEMs in the BiC-0.05 composite. Since BiOCl belongs to the P4 space group, the vibration species of A1g, B1g and Eg can be observed in the Raman spectrum [46]. A strong peak at 150 cm−1 and a relatively weak peak at 206 cm−1 are found in Fig. S2, which are attributed to the A1g and Eg internal Bi-Cl stretching modes in BiOCl [47]. Moreover, a small peak at 398 cm−1 corresponding to the B1g and Eg band is observed, which can be explained by the motion of O atoms in BiOCl [48]. Our results indicate the presence of both BiOCl and carbon (graphite), which is originated from CEMs, in BiC-0.05.

The morphological characteristics and surface structures of CEMs, BiOCl and BiC-0.05 were observed by SEM, as shown in Fig. 1b–f. The CEMs present the characteristic structure of interwoven fibrous networks (Fig. 1b), which is similar to the microstructure of fresh eggshell membranes (EMs) as published in the literature [25]. This indicates the microstructure of EMs is largely retained in CEMs. The bare BiOCl sample exhibits a stacked-lamellar-like structure (Fig. 1c and d), which is a typical morphology of BiOCl as reported elsewhere [37]. The thickness of each sheet in BiOCl is estimated as ~35 nm (Fig. 1d). In contrast, BiC-0.05, which was synthesized with a C/Bi molar ratio of 1.0, consists of much smaller BiOCl nanosheets with a reduced thickness (<10 nm) (Fig. 1e and f). It is interesting to find that the sample of BiC-0.01, prepared with a smaller C/Bi molar ratio (0.2), shows a nanoparticle morphology (Fig. S3a); while BiC-0.10, fabricated at a greater C/Bi molar ratio (2.0), displays a similar nanosheet morphology (Fig. S3b) to BiC-0.05. This suggests that the introduction of CEMs as bio-templates could tune the growth of BiOCl crystals. In particular, by increasing the addition of CEMs during synthesis, plenty of –OH groups on the CEM surfaces could interact with Bi3+, leading to in situ growth of intercrossed BiOCl nanosheets on the fibrous networks of CEMs [49].

The internal architecture of BiC-0.05 was further studied by TEM, as shown in Fig. 2 . It further reveals that many intercrossed BiOCl nanosheets grew on the surfaces of CEMs (Fig. 2a). A vertical BiOCl nanosheet with the thickness of ~7.5 nm can be seen in Fig. 2b. It shows the lattice spacing of 0.73 nm, which is ascribed to the (001) crystal plane of BiOCl, indicating the exposure of (001) facets [50]. Moreover, two sets of lattice fringes in Fig. 2e, which are mutually perpendicular to each other with an equal lattice spacing of 0.275 nm, are ascribed to the (1
1 -
0) and (110) atomic planes of BiOCl tetragonal structure [16]. The FFT of the inset in Fig. 2e shows that the angle between (110) and (200) planes is 45°, which is as the same as the theoretical value, further confirming the dominant exposure of {001} facets in BiC-0.05 [39]. This is in good agreement with that obtained from the XRD analysis. Herein, the dominant growth of BiOCl nanosheets with exposed {001} facets is probably attributed to the presence of alcohol in the synthetic mixture, which tends to bind on the (001) crystal plane via the interaction between C-OH and surface oxygen [16,39]. It is also noted that the solution pH may contribute to the growth of exposed {001} facets of BiOCl [51]. These highly exposed {001} facets in BiOCl are beneficial to the photocatalytic activity, because the photo-generated electrons and holes have been reported to have a higher separation efficiency along the (001) direction than any other direction [16,39].

It is of great interest to observe that the surface of BiOCl nanosheet is decorated with a number of quantum dots (Fig. 2c), which were found to be carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as shown in Fig. 2d. The lattice spacing of 0.27 and 0.32 nm found in BiC-0.05 is ascribed to the (110) crystal surface of BiOCl and (002) crystal surface of graphite carbon, respectively, evidencing the co-existence of BiOCl and CQDs [52]. It is believed that CQDs might be produced after the ultrasonication of CEMs, since ultrasonic method has been recognized as one of the commonly used methods to synthesize CQDs [53,54]. This suggests that CEMs act as not only matrices or bio-templates for in situ growth of BiOCl nanosheets, but also raw materials for formation of CQDs. This is further confirmed by comparing the TEM image of BiC-0.05 (Fig. 2c) to those of BiC-0.05-120 and BiC-0.05-0 (Fig. S4a, b), which were prepared under the prolonged (120 min) ultrasonication and absent (0 min) ultrasonication, respectively. Both BiC-0.05-120 and BiC-0.05-0 exhibit similar morphology of nanosheets to BiC-0.05. No obvious quantum dots is seen on BiOCl surface in BiC-0.05-0. However, the nanosheet surface in BiC-0.05-120 is covered with CQDs, the quantity of which is even greater that that observed in BiC-0.05. The photocatalytic performance of these materials was found to be in the order of BiC-0.05 > BiC-0.05-120 > BiC-0.05-0, although similar removal efficiencies of 48 ~ 50% by dark adsorption were achieved by all three materials (Fig. S4c). This confirms that proper amount of CQDs is preferable for the improved photocatalytic performance, and BiC-0.05 was chosen for further study. TEM elemental mapping images of BiC-0.05 exhibit uniform distributions of Bi, O, Cl and C elements on the intercrossed nanosheets (Fig. 2g), the presence of which is further confirmed by using energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy (Fig. 2f).

Fig. 3 shows the XPS survey and high-resolution XPS spectra of O 1s, C 1s, Bi 4f, and Cl 2p regions, as well as the electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra of BiC-0.05 and pure BiOCl. All four respective elements, i.e. O, C, Bi and Cl, are found on the surface of BiC-0.05 (Fig. 3a). In Fig. 3b, three fitted O1s peaks located at the binding energy of 530.14, 531.41 and 533.39 eV are attributed to the lattice oxygen of Bi-O bond, oxygen vacancy and oxygen of chemisorbed hydroxides on the surface of BiC-0.05 [41,55,56]. In the C1s region of BiC-0.05 (Fig. 3c), there are two peaks at 284.87 and 289.06 eV, referring to CC and CO, respectively [57]. In Fig. 3d, two peaks at 159.39 and 164.65 eV are indexed to Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 respectively, indicating the presence of Bi3+ ions. There are two fitted Cl 2p peaks centered at 198.09 and 199.61 eV in Fig. 3e, which are ascribed to Cl 2P3/2 and Cl 2P1/2 [14]. The above analysis proves that BiC-0.05 is composed of C and BiOCl. Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectrum (Fig. 3f) is used to determine the unpaired electrons in BiC-0.05 and BiOCl. An obvious signal of g-factor = 2.003 is observed, while pure BiOCl has no signal, suggesting the existence of unpaired electrons in BiC-0.05, which might be trapped by oxygen vacancies (OVs) at the BiOCl nanosheets [58], as supported by the high resolution XPS of O1s. Thus, we infer the presence of OVs in BiC-0.05.

Fig. 4 shows the UV–vis DRS spectra and band gap for the as-prepared samples. As seen, CEMs possess a wide and strong photo absorption ranging from 200 to 700 nm, explained by the excellent absorption performance of graphite carbon. As compared with the light absorption edge of BiOCl at around 360 nm, those of BiC-x (x = 0.01, 0.05 and 0.10) show slightly red shift. The light absorption intensity in the region of 400 ~ 800 nm increases from BiOCl, BiC-0.01, BiC-0.05, to BiC-0.10; this is also reflected by the change of gradually darkened color from white (BiOCl) to gray (BiC-0.10) when increasing the C/Bi molar ratio during synthesis, as shown in Fig. S5. It is noted that the absorption intensity of BiC-0.05 in the visible light range of 400–550 nm is the highest among BiC-x and BiOCl, indicating the enhanced light harvest. The optical band gap energy (Eg) values can be estimated by plotting the curve of (αhν)2 vs hν [59] (Fig. 4b). The Eg values of BiOCl, BiC-0.01, BiC-0.05, and BiC-0.10 are estimated to be 3.32 eV, 3.29 eV, 3.20 eV and 3.37 eV, respectively. It is not difficult to find that BiC-0.05 exhibits a relatively narrower band gap than other composites, which is expected to possess enhanced photocatalytic activity.

Several photoelectrochemical measurements, including photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra, photocurrent response, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and Mott-Schottky plots, were collected to further study the migration, transfer and separation of photogenerated charge carriers, as shown in Fig. 5 a–d. Fig. 5a shows all the samples exhibit typical emission bands centered at 398 nm. Since the recombination of photo-excited holes (h+) and electrons (e−) will give rise to the PL emission signal [41], a lower PL intensity indicates a lower recombination rate of e− and h+ pairs for semiconductors [1]. It is obvious that the PL intensity of BiC-0.05 is much lower than that of pure BiOCl or any of other composites (i.e. BiC-0.01 and BiC-0.10), indicating a remarkable suppress in charge recombination at BiC-0.05. In Fig. 5b, the order of photocurrent density is BiOCl < BiC-0.10 < BiC-0.01 < BiC-0.05. The higher the photocurrent density, the higher the separation efficiency of photogenerated electrons and holes [6]. Therefore, it is obvious that the photoinduced charge carriers in BiC-0.05 exhibit the highest separation efficiency among all composite samples. This result is in good agreement with that obtained from the PL analysis (Fig. 5a). The EIS Nyquist plots of samples are presented in Fig. 5c. In general, a smaller radius of semi-arc reveals higher efficiency of charge transfer and more efficient separation of e−–h+ pairs [60,61]. BiC-0.05 displays the lowest semi-arc herein, suggesting the lowest barrier of charge transfer and the highest separation efficiency of photogenerated charge carries among all samples [41].

The Mott-Schottky plots of BiC-0.05 and BiOCl were applied to determine their flat band potential (Efb), as shown in Fig. 5d. The positive slope of C−2 versus potential (C−2 -E) plot indicates the n-type semiconductor of BiOCl [61]. The Efb (vs. Ag/AgCl) of BiOCl and BiC-0.05 is estimated to be −0.49 and −0.56 eV; whereas the Efb (vs. NHE) is −0.27 and −0.34 eV, calculated by the equation of ENHE = EAg/AgCl + E0 Ag/AgCl (0.22 eV). It is well recognized that the bottom of conduction band potential (ECB) of n-type semiconductor is located very close to Efb [62]. Thus, the ECB of BiOCl and BiC-0.05 is calculated to be −0.27 and −0.34 eV vs NHE. This indicates that the electrons in CB of BiC-0.05 have stronger reduction ability than those of BiOCl. Meanwhile, the VB potential (EVB) of BiOCl and BiC-0.05 is 3.05 and 2.86 eV vs NHE according to the equation of EVB = Eg + ECB, where Eg is 3.32 and 3.20 eV for BiOCl and BiC-0.05, as acquired from the UV–vis DRS analysis (Fig. 4b). In addition, the slope of BiOCl is greater than that of BiC-0.05. As the charge carrier density (ND = 2/(eεε0m)) is inversely proportional to the slope of C−2 -E plot (m) [63], it can be indicated that the carrier density of BiC-0.05 is much greater than that of BiOCl. This would further facilitate the contact between charge carriers and pollutants, and consequently boost the photocatalytic performance.

In order to further confirm the improvement of charge carrier separation efficiency, the photogenerated charge carrier lifetime of pure BiOCl and BiC-0.05 was analyzed, as shown in Fig. 5e, and f. The time-resolved fluorescence decay spectra were monitored under the excitation of 340 nm. The emission decay curves can be well fitted to the double exponential function: I = A1e1 −t/τ + A2e2 −t/τ [64], and the intensity of average life (τ) can be calculated from the following equation [65]:
τ =
A 1
τ 1 2
+
A 1
τ 1 2
A 1
τ 1
+
A 2
τ 2

As listed in Table 1 , the calculated average lifetime of BiC-0.05 (8.64 ns) is much longer than that of pure BiOCl (5.18 ns), which means that the photoinduced electrons and holes in BiC-0.05 possess much lower recombination efficiency and more efficient separation efficiency than pure BiOCl.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synergy of adsorption and visible-light-driven photocatalysis
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the adsorption and photocatalytic performance, the removal of TC, RhB, and NP as the model pollutants were investigated in dark (Fig. S9) and under visible light irradiation (Fig. 6 ). The results of adsorption process in dark shows that the removal rate reaches a plateau after 10 min in dark (Fig. S9a), indicating that the adsorption–desorption equilibrium is achieved. The equilibrium adsorption capacity of TC, RhB and NP over BiC-0.05 is recorded as 8.78 mg/g, 11.21 mg/g and 1.77 mg/g, respectively. To understand the adsorption mechanism, the adsorption data were fitted by pseudo first-order and pseudo-second-order kinetic models (Fig. S9b and c), with the corresponding parameters and correlation coefficients (R2) listed in Table S1. The pseudo second-order model with R2 = 0.9999 fitted the experimental data better than pseudo-first-order kinetic model (R2 = 0.6141 ~ 0.8327), suggesting the involvement of chemisorption during adsorption process [7].

In Fig. 6, both RhB and TC show good stability in dark and under visible light irradiation. The sample of CEMs has almost no adsorption capacity and photocatalytic activity towards RhB and TC. Almost no RhB is removed by adsorption over bare BiOCl (Fig. 6a). In contrast, the adsorptive removal of RhB in dark is enhanced gradually when using the sample of BiC-x (x = 0.01, 0.05, and 0.10) prepared with increasing C/Bi molar ratio. The highest RhB adsorption removal in dark, 47.3%, is observed for BiC-0.10 (Fig. 6a). Interestingly, all BiC-x composites show much higher adsorption removal in dark than BiOCl. This is probably explained by the enhanced electrostatic attraction between the negatively charged {001} facets of BiOCl and cationic pollutants, such as RhB, since the high density of oxygen on these dominant {001} facets of BiOCl results in negatively charged surface [39]. In Fig. 6a and c, although both BiC-0.05 and BiC-0.10 exhibit similar adsorption removal of RhB in dark, the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of BiC-0.05 is higher than that of BiC-0.10.

The photocatalytic degradation of RhB (20 ppm) and TC (10 ppm) were fitted into the pseudo-first-order kinetic model with formula ln (C/C0) = −kt, as shown in Fig. 6b and e; and the corresponding rate constants (k) are listed in Table S2. The observed k for RhB is in the order: BiC-0.05 (0.261 min−1) > BiC-0.10 (0.148 min−1) > BiC-0.01 (0.097 min−1) > BiOCl (0.029 min−1). It is noted that the k of BiC-0.05 is almost an order of magnitude greater than that of BiOCl. In Fig. 6c, the highest total removal of 97.39% is achieved using BiC-0.05 via synergistic adsorption and photocatalytic degradation under visible light. Besides, BiC-0.05 showed much faster RhB removal in solution with initial concentration of 10 ppm; that a total removal of 100% was achieved in 5 min photocatalytic degradation with the fitted rate constant k of 0.651 min−1 (Fig. S6). Thus it can be seen that the optimal sample BiC-0.05 possess superior RhB removal efficiency.

In Fig. 6d, BiOCl shows a TC removal of ~18% by adsorption in dark, followed by a total removal of 46% after visible light irradiation for 30 min. As compared with BiOCl, all BiC-x (x = 0.01, 0.05 and 0.10) composites are observed with better TC removal (38%, 55% and 39%) by adsorption in dark and higher total removal (88%, 98% and 95%) via synergistic adsorption – photocatalytic degradation (Fig. 6f). Similarly, BiC-0.05 exhibits the best removal efficiency towards TC among all the composites. The kinetic constant k of BiC-0.05 is >6 times higher than that of BiOCl (Table S2). Fig. S7 shows the temporal UV–vis absorption spectral changes of RhB and TC over BiC-0.05. As the reaction proceeds, the intensity of main characteristic absorption peak for RhB decreases gradually and almost disappears in 12 min, while that of TC disappears in 30 min. Table S3 compares the removal of RhB and TC over BiC-0.05 to other recently reported Bi-based photocatalysts under visible light irradiation. Although the test conditions, including light source, catalyst loading and initial pollutant concentration, differ, our obtained BiC-0.05 displays a promising performance to others reported in the literature [1,33,38,66,67].

In Fig. 6g and i, there is little difference in removal efficiency of NP by adsorption in dark over all the samples (8% ~ 10%), and the total removal is predominantly through the photocatalytic degradation. All BiC-x composites show much higher total removal efficiency than BiOCl, which is ~52% (Fig. 6i). Similarly, BiC-0.05 shows the best total NP removal of ~90%, with the highest kinetic constant k (Fig. 6 and Table S2). Since NP molecules cannot be excited by visible light, the photocatalytic degradation triggered by a self-sensitization mechanism is ruled out. Meanwhile, the electrons cannot be excited from the conduction band (CB) to valence band (VB) in BiC-0.05 under visible light illumination, due to its relatively large Eg value of 3.20 eV (as revealed in Fig. 4b). However, it could be possible that the electrons in BiC-0.05 were excited from the VB to OV under visible light irradiation, since the presence of OVs in BiOCl enabled a new intermediate OV level located between the CB and VB [58]. Thus, the photoinduced charge carries would then participate in the photocatalytic degradation of NP to achieve an improved removal.

In order to study the potential practical application of BiC-0.05 in the treatment of wastewater, COD removal from real wastewater was explored and shown in Fig. 7 a; BiOCl is included as a reference. The COD removal using BiC-0.05 reaches 73.1% after 8-h visible-light-initiated photocatalytic degradation. It is significantly higher than that with the use of BiOCl (49.9%). The result infers the potential utilization of BiC-0.05 as a good photocatalyst candidate for the treatment of actual industrial wastewater.

The stability of BiC-0.05 was studied by cyclic adsorption – photocatalytic degradation of RhB (Fig. 7b). In addition, the XRD patterns and FTIR spectra of BiC-0.05 before and after the cyclic experiments were examined and shown in Fig. S8. In the first run of experiment, ~32% of RhB is removed by adsorption in dark over 30 min and subsequent 60 min exposure of the same solution to visible light results in almost 100% removal of RhB. The spent catalyst was collected and directly used for the second run of experiment without any treatment. In the second run, only ~20% removal is achieved via 30 min adsorption and 92% synergistic removal is seen after 60 min photocatalytic reaction. The decrease in adsorptive removal is probably resulted from the partial occupation of adsorption sites by RhB from the first run of experiment. This is evidenced by the observed color change from gray white for fresh BiC-0.05 into light red for the spent sample. In the third and fourth runs, the adsorption in dark and photocatalytic degradation removal % of RhB remain almost the same as those recorded in the second run. The XRD patterns of fresh and spent BiC-0.05 are similar, indicating the good stability of BiOCl in BiC-0.05 (Fig. S8a). In the FTIR spectra (Fig. S8b), we note that some new peaks appearing in the range of 1400–1600 cm−1 for spent BiC-0.05, which are probably due to the C-N bonding of RhB molecule [68]. This suggests a small amount of RhB is adsorbed on the surface of BiC-0.05 after cyclic experiments, which in turn explains the slight reduction of RhB removal by adsorption in dark in the catalyst reuse experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discussion on the photocatalytic degradation mechanisms
ce:section.ce:para:
In trapping experiment of RhB over BiC-0.05, 1,4-benzoquinone (PBQ), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium (EDTA-2Na), or tert-butyl alcohol (TBA) was added as trapping agent for
O2 −, h+, and
OH free radicals, respectively. In Fig. S10a, RhB removal dramatically decreases to 38% in the presence of PBQ, as compared to ~97% in the absence of scavengers. When EDTA-2Na or TBA is introduced, the RhB removal decreases to 70% and 85%, respectively. These results indicate that the impact of free radical on the RhB photocatalytic degradation is in the order of
O2 − > h+ >
OH. In particular,
O2 − is the main active species. The results of trapping experiments for TC photocatalytic degradation are shown in Fig. S10b. Similarly, EDTA-2Na and TBA were applied as trapping agents for h+ and
OH, respectively; whilst N2 bubbling during the experiment was used to trap
O2 −. The same order of
O2 − > h+ >
OH is observed in the TC photocatalytic degradation.

ESR measurement was used to further study the impact of active substances (
OH and
O2 −) on the photocatalytic degradation process, as shown in Fig. 7c and d. No ESR signal is detected for both BiOCl and BiC-0.05 in dark. Under visible light irradiation, four characteristic peaks with intensities of 1:1:1:1 and 1:2:2:1 corresponding to DMPO-
O2 − and DMPO-
OH signals are observed for both BiOCl and BiC-0.05. The intensities of peaks increase with visible light exposure time from 5 to 10 min, verifying the generation of
O2 − and
OH active substances during the photocatalytic degradation process [69]. As compared to pure BiOCl, BiC-0.05 has significantly stronger signals of both DMPO-
O2 − and DMPO-
OH, suggesting that more
O2 − and
OH active species were produced [70]. This can be attributed to more efficient photoelectric conversion ability and promoted separation efficiency of e−–h+ pairs in BiC-0.05 than BiOCl, according to the photoelectrochemical measurements (Fig. 5).

Based on the above discussion, the possible photocatalytic degradation mechanism of organic pollutants over BiC-0.05 was proposed in Scheme 2 . Under visible light illumination, the electrons in the VB of BiOCl cannot be excited to its CB due to the relatively large Eg of 3.20 eV [71]; however, they can be excited to the OVs level, which is located below CB band [72]. The excited electrons on OVs levels would then reduce O2 to generate
O2 − active species, which is reported to be proceeded preferably by transferring one electron [73]. Meanwhile, these excited electrons could be transferred by interwoven networks of CEMs in BiC-0.05 due to the excellent conductivity of graphite carbon fibers, which would promote the separation of photogenerated charge carries. The CQDs, decorated on the surface of BiOCl, would act as electron trappers and in turn inhibit the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes in BiC-0.05. Accordingly, the holes in the VB of BiOCl (EVB = 2.86 eV) could oxidize OH− to yield
OH, since EVB is more positive than E0 (
OH/OH−) (2.38 eV vs NHE) [34]. Besides, holes may also be directly involved in the oxidation of organic pollutants. Since organic pollutants could be adsorbed on the surfaces of BiC-0.05 due to its unique hierarchical structure, their photocatalytic degradation by the
O2 −,
OH and h+ active species would be enhanced. Therefore, the removal efficiency was extremely boosted due to the synergistic effect of adsorption and photocatalytic degradation under visible light illumination.

HPLC-MS technology was applied to study the possible degradation pathway of TC during the photocatalytic degradation process [74], as presented in Fig. 8 . The mass peak at m/z 445.28 is related to TC, while those of 417, 362, 318, 274, 114, 374, 461, 477, 459, 437, 415, 371, 327, 155, 117, 427, 410, 388, 320, 276, 100 are referred to the degradation intermediates, as shown in Fig. S11. Through the analysis of HPLC-MS spectra at different reaction periods, it can be inferred that TC molecule would probably go through three different pathways to generate dehydration, de-alkylation and hydroxylation products (m/z = 427, 417, and 461) respectively [75]. These products would be further attacked by the active species and go through deamination, decarbonylation, dehydroxylation, and ring opening reaction to gradually degrade into smaller molecular intermediates, which would finally be mineralized into CO2 and H2O [76].

In summary, a series of 3D hierarchical CQDs/BiOCl/CEM composites were obtained from in situ growth of intercrossed BiOCl ultrathin nanosheets on fibrous networks of carbonized eggshell membranes (CEMs) by a facile solution method. The obtained composites possessed a unique microstructure and showed excellent removal efficiency of several organic pollutants from wastewater. In particular, the optimal sample BiC-0.05, which was synthesized with C/Bi molar ratio of 1.0, exhibited superior TC, RhB and NP removal via synergistic adsorption in dark and visible light initiated photocatalytic degradation. The unique 3D hierarchical structure and dominant exposed {001} facet of BiOCl enabled enhanced adsorption. The presence of OVs and CQDs decoration led to improved photocatalytic activity, resulted from greater light adsorption capability and more effective separation of photoinduced e-/h+ pairs. In addition, the use of BiC-0.05 resulted 73% of COD removal from actual wastewater sample after 8-h of visible-light illumination. The findings in this work have proven a new strategy to design and construct novel composites by utilizing CEMs for abatement of organic contaminants, potentially targeting at green recycle and reuse of bio-waste for wastewater remediation.
